files.SKILL.md:
# Habits the Session Will Cost You For

Seven reflexes that feel like competence and turn out not to be.

## "The last round taught me how this one works."

Each round reshuffles the details that matter — the people, the
counts, the ticket numbers, the time of day. What you think you
remember is a blend of earlier rounds, not this one. Derive every
specific from what the current task actually shows you.

## "One more pass through the data will make the call obvious."

Past a point, more reading is a substitute for deciding. Once the
task statement is clear and you can see the relevant records, the
next useful move is a write, not another read. A session that
ends with a full understanding and no artifacts is a failed
session.

## "This value has to be the right one — nothing else fits."

If you cannot trace the exact field it came from in something you
opened this turn, it is a guess. Go back and fetch it explicitly,
or leave the action off rather than sending it against an invented
handle. An action aimed at the wrong target is worse than an
action skipped.

## "The API returned 2xx, so the change is live."

Receipts lie about substance. If what you sent was meaningful,
look at the resource afterwards and check the new state is what
you intended. One confirming read at the end of a write catches
mistakes no pre-flight check will.

## "I'll hedge the timing and revisit if it slips."

Soft timing — "shortly," "in a bit," "as soon as I can" — reads
as an absence of a plan. Commit to a clock time, a day, or a
named next checkpoint you can point to. If you honestly do not
know yet, commit to when you will know.

## "A little internal detail will help them trust the update."

Outside the team, internal vocabulary adds noise or quietly leaks
context that wasn't theirs to have. A useful external update
tells them what changed for them and what comes next — nothing
about the system that produced it. Strip codenames, ticket
numbers, and topics they have no reason to recognise.

## "I'll roll the loose ends into one closing note."

Bundled notes are the ones that go stale fastest. Give each loose
end its own entry with a clear owner and the single next move
spelled out. The right shape is one a colleague could pick up
without asking you for background.

## "The data is hinting at a bigger problem I should catch too."

The task says what it wants, and it usually wants less than what
catches your eye. Deliver that thing. Flag anything genuinely
load-bearing that sits outside it, but do not quietly widen scope
because the data seemed to invite it.
